elements.sent-2411-001: Right: We demonstrate the efficacy of \model~in handling complex scenarios involving compositional object understanding and task-aware tool using under targeted human instructions, including: (a) attributes and relations like color, size, material, (b) tool function reasoning according to action, (c) common tool using in complex scenes.. \end{center ].}
elements.sent-2411-002: Inferring affordable (i.e., graspable) parts of arbitrary objects based on human specifications is essential for robots advancing toward open-vocabulary manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-003: Current grasp planners, however, are hindered by limited vision-language comprehension and time-consuming 3D radiance modeling, restricting real-time, open-vocabulary interactions with objects.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address these limitations, we propose GLOVER, a unified Generalizable Open-Vocabulary Affordance Reasoning framework, which fine-tunes the Large Language Models (LLMs) to predict visual affordance of graspable object parts within RGB feature space.
elements.sent-2411-005: We compile a dataset of over 10,000 images from human-object interactions, annotated with unified visual and linguistic affordance labels, to enable multi-modal fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-006: GLOVER inherits world knowledge and common-sense reasoning from LLMs, facilitating more fine-grained object understanding and sophisticated tool-use reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-007: To enable effective real-world deployment, we present Affordance-Aware Grasping Estimation (AGE), a non-parametric grasp planner that aligns the gripper pose with a superquadric surface derived from affordance data.
elements.sent-2411-008: In evaluations across 30 real-world scenes, GLOVER achieves success rates of 86.0\% in part identification and 76.3\% in grasping, with speeds approximately 330 times faster in affordance reasoning and 40 times faster in grasping pose estimation than the previous state-of-the-art.
elements.sent-2411-009: Human beings have an inherent ability to manipulate objects by understanding natural language instructions, such as distinguishing between different object types, identifying objects’ locations, and determining which part to grasp based on the desired task.
elements.sent-2411-010: Motivated by this, research in robotic grasping has evolved from focusing on closed-set objects~\cite{peract, goyal2023rvt, ma2024contrastive to open-vocabulary methods~\cite{kokic2020learning, lerftogo, zheng2024gaussiangrasper, geff, roboabc, okrobot, padalkar2023open, huang2024a3vlm, huang2023voxposer .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, these open-vocabulary grasping methods suffer from a lack of complex reasoning capabilities regarding object properties.
elements.sent-2411-012: They face challenges in locating objects with subtle linguistic distinctions (e.g., distinguishing between a red apple and a green apple as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:intro ) and determining task-specific graspable parts (e.g., pick up the pot versus open the pot as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:intro ).}}
elements.sent-2411-013: For example, LERF-TOGO~\cite{lerftogo relies on offline large language models (LLMs) to infer the graspable part based on the task (hammer handle), as we show in the left of Fig.~\ref{fig:intro .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Despite this, it still fails to accurately identify grasp locations due to limited vision-language comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-015: Additionally, many previous approaches~\cite{lerf, lerftogo, zheng2024gaussiangrasper, huang2023voxposer, geff depend heavily on 3D radiance modeling~\cite{nerf, gaussiansplat to construct 3D feature fields, which is both time-consuming and restricts their generalizability to new scenes.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: To tackle the aforementioned challenges, we present a unified Generalizable Open-Vocabulary Affordance Reasoning (\model) framework for open-vocabulary robotic grasping.
elements.sent-2411-017: This proposed framework aims to address three questions: 1.
elements.sent-2411-018: How to represent the graspable part? Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong open-vocabulary reasoning capabilities and it is promising to fine-tune LLMs to output visual affordance masks.
elements.sent-2411-019: To achieve this, we define the graspable region as a global visual affordance mask, inspired by the visual affordance inferring~\cite{hou2021affordance, luo2022learning, affcorrs, affordancellm and reasoning segmentation task~\cite{lai2024lisa, groundingdino, rasheed2024glamm .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Unlike a binary mask, this affordance mask encodes a continuous probability map, representing the likelihood of grasping at various locations.
elements.sent-2411-021: We adopt the affordance mask representation for two reasons: (1) graspable parts are better represented as regions rather than single points, and (2) predicting global masks based on language input aligns more naturally with model decoding~\cite{llava, sam, lai2024lisa, he2022masked .}
elements.sent-2411-022: To support this approach, we compile a dataset of over 10,000 human-object interaction images, unifying the linguistic annotations and visual affordance annotation using a Gaussian distribution centered on graspable points.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-023: How to empower the model with complex reasoning and world knowledge to handle linguistic instructions? With the collected dataset, we fine-tune the model to generate visual affordance masks.
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, the fine-tuning process takes images and language instructions as input, leveraging LLaVA-7B~\cite{llava to perform multi-modal encoding following~\cite{lai2024lisa .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The encoded affordance token, which aggregates both visual and linguistic features, is fed into an affordance decoder to output the desired affordance mask.
elements.sent-2411-026: This mask is then projected into 3D space as stereo affordance for downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-027: How to deploy the model efficiently in real robots? We introduce an Affordance-Aware Grasping Estimation (\age) module that estimates gripper poses based on the geometry of affordance regions for real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-028: Inspired by~\cite{liu2022robust, vezzani2017grasping , \age~samples grasping poses within the affordance space, aligning the gripper with the superquadric surface derived from the stereo affordance geometry.}
elements.sent-2411-029: This approach eliminates the need for conventional grasping planners, enabling direct, affordance-aware pose estimation.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our \model~module achieves state-of-the-art performance in the affordance benchmark, surpassing previous methods by a significant margin.
elements.sent-2411-031: To validate real-world applicability, we deploy \model~in a physical robot system, testing its open-vocabulary grasping capabilities across 30 challenging object scenes.
elements.sent-2411-032: Additionally, it achieves approximately $\times$330 faster affordance reasoning and $\times$40 faster grasping pose estimation.

elements.sent-2411-056: In this section, we tackle two key problems for open-vocabulary affordance reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-057: First, we identify an effective approach to represent visual affordance for fine-tuning the foundational model (Sec.~\ref{sec:data ).}
elements.sent-2411-058: Second, we examine how to integrate affordance knowledge into an existing foundational model while preserving as much of its original world knowledge as possible (Sec.\ref{sec:3.2 -~\ref{sec:3.4 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-060: To enable affordance reasoning with the world knowledge of LLMs, we leverage the abundant resources of 2D images with visual affordance annotations.
elements.sent-2411-061: We select images from two common affordance datasets, AGD20K~\cite{agd20k and 3DOI~\cite{3doi .}}
elements.sent-2411-062: AGD20K is a semi-supervised affordance dataset that includes 23,816 images with 50 categories of objects and 36 categories of affordance, sourced mainly from COCO~\cite{lin2014coco , HICO~\cite{chao2015hico and free-license websites.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: For our purposes, we only use the testset split of AGD20K with well-annotated visual affordance points and corresponding text labels.
elements.sent-2411-064: For 3DOI, we use 10,000 images drawn from Articulation~\cite{articulation , EpicKitchen~\cite{epickitchen , and Taskonomy~\cite{zamir2018taskonomy , which contain over 5,000 affordance-related interaction points.}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: However, 3DOI does not include text labels for the interactable objects.
elements.sent-2411-066: Both AGD20K and 3DOI images feature a mix of egocentric and exocentric views.
elements.sent-2411-067: We collect the linguistic and visual affordance annotations as follows.
elements.sent-2411-069: To address missing object labels in the 3DOI~\cite{3doi dataset, we utilize VLMs for linguistic annotating, followed by human cross-validation to correct any errors.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Specifically, we first crop objects from the background based on the bounding box annotations of the images.
elements.sent-2411-071: The cropped object images are then fed into a vision-language foundation model, SEED-X-17B~\cite{ge2024seed , and prompt it with ``What is the object in the image?".}
elements.sent-2411-072: We manually filter the generated answers to retain only relevant object categories to ensure accurate annotations.
elements.sent-2411-075: Visual affordances represent the interaction points where humans engage with objects.
elements.sent-2411-076: Predicting affordance through global masks from language inputs aligns more naturally with model decoding.
elements.sent-2411-077: Hence, following the approach in 3DOI~\cite{3doi , we transform affordance points into a 2D Gaussian bump representation.}
elements.sent-2411-078: For human-object interaction points in a 2D image, represented as $a_k = [x_k, y_k]$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$, we define the affordance probability mask as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:gaussian} \boldsymbol{\hat{M}}_{aff}(i, j) = {\rm exp}\left(-\frac{(i-x_k)^2 + (j-y_k)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right),\end{equation} where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation, and $(i, j)\in [0,W]\times[0,H]$ is the spatial indice within the image.. elements.sent-2411-081: Each instance is annotated with both visual and linguistic affordance labels.

elements.sent-2411-133: We evaluate \model~on a wide range of objects from diverse scenes, focusing on two main challenges: compositional object understanding, and task-aware tool using.
elements.sent-2411-134: Compositional object understanding refers to the agent's understanding of the fine-grained object attributes like colors, sizes, materials and relations in a compositional way.

elements.sent-2411-194: In conclusion, our \model~framework demonstrates substantial advancements in open-vocabulary affordance reasoning and task-oriented grasping, outperforming existing methods in both accuracy and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-195: By fine-tuning a foundational model with enhanced affordance understanding while preserving world knowledge, \model~achieves state-of-the-art performance across real-world grasping and affordance reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-196: Our proposed \age~module enables rapid and precise grasping pose estimation, overcoming limitations in speed and adaptability seen in prior approaches.
elements.sent-2411-197: Extensive experiments and ablation studies confirm \model’s capabilities in complex reasoning, efficient affordance inference, and robust grasping across diverse scenarios, positioning it as a versatile tool for real-world robotic manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-198: Despite the promises, several limitations remained.
elements.sent-2411-199: First, our primary focus is on grasping tasks, with limited demonstration of effectiveness in long-horizon manipulation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-200: For long-horizon tasks, \model~decomposes into multiple steps, potentially resulting in less natural long-horizon behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-201: Second, the \age~cannot estimate the tactile information for the grasping, which limits the ability of grasping fragile objects like glass.
elements.sent-2411-202: In the future, we aim to improve the \model~from the above two aspects.

elements.sent-2411-226: In this work, we present a unified Generalizable Open-Vocabulary Affordance Reasoning (\model) framework for open-vocabulary robotic grasping in an end-to-end manner.
elements.sent-2411-227: We aim to leverage the open-vocabulary reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) and fine-tune LLMs to output visual affordance masks.
elements.sent-2411-228: To support this approach, we collect over 10,000 human-object interaction images, using Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to annotate the language labels and unified Gaussian distribution to annotate visual affordance masks.
elements.sent-2411-229: With the collected dataset, we take the pairs of image and language instruction as input, leveraging LLaVA-7B~\cite{llava to perform multi-modal encoding following~\cite{lai2024lisa .}}
elements.sent-2411-230: The fine-tuning pipeline of \model~offers two key benefits: (1) It can leverage extensive 2D human-object interaction images from diverse kinds of datasets~\cite{grauman2022ego4d, epickitchen, 3doi, agd20k , overcoming the scarcity of 3D affordance data.}
elements.sent-2411-231: To enable real-world grasping, we introduce an Affordance-Aware Grasping Estimation (\age) module that estimates gripper poses based on the geometry of affordance regions.
elements.sent-2411-232: Inspired by~\cite{liu2022robust, vezzani2017grasping , \age~samples grasping poses within the affordance space, determining the target pose by aligning the gripper with the superquadric surface derived from the stereo affordance geometry The alignment is optimized via nonlinear constrained optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-233: This approach eliminates the usage of standalone grasping pose models~\cite{kuang2024ram,roboabc , enabling direct and affordance-aware pose estimation in an end-to-end manner.}
elements.sent-2411-234: To summarize, our contributions are as follows: (i) We present \model, a unified end-to-end perception framework designed for open-vocabulary robotic grasping.
elements.sent-2411-235: To enhance its capabilities, we have curated and annotated a dataset of over 10,000 multi-modal affordance images for fine-tuning, enabling \model~to leverage world knowledge and common-sense reasoning inherited from large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-236: We test the open-vocabulary grasping capability across 30 challenging table-top real-world scenes, demonstrating an average improvement of $20.0\%$ in affordance reasoning success rate and $17.3\%$ in grasping success rate compared to the previous state-of-the-art.
elements.sent-2411-237: We also demonstrate the generalization across diverse scenes and embodiments, showing effectiveness in 4 tasks with humanoid robots and dexterous hands.
